content.text: Template Versioning and Upgrade Path

A GenAI starter template is not a one-time artifact — it is a living standard. Platform teams continuously improve the scaffold: adding eval harnesses, hardening security defaults, updating LLM client patterns, and incorporating lessons from production incidents. Without a versioning strategy, these improvements only benefit newly scaffolded services. Services scaffolded six months ago remain on outdated patterns indefinitely.

This sub-subtopic covers how to version the template repository, how to communicate breaking changes, and how to build an upgrade path that existing services can follow without requiring a complete re-scaffold.
Versioning the Template Repository
Semantic Versioning for Templates
Template repositories follow semantic versioning, but the semantics apply to the scaffold's conventions rather than its code:
Version bump
Meaning
Examples
Patch (1.0.x)
Non-breaking fixes and documentation improvements
Fix typo in mkdocs.yml, update dependency version in pyproject.toml.njk
Minor (1.x.0)
Additive changes; services can opt in without any breaking risk
Add optional RAG module, add streaming endpoint skeleton, add new workflow
Major (x.0.0)
Breaking changes to structure, config schema, or required conventions
Rename src/models/ to src/llm/, restructure eval config, new required catalog annotation
Tags on the template repository (
) allow Backstage Software Templates to pin to a specific version via
in the template's
fetch:template
action input. Services are effectively pinned to the template version they were scaffolded from — the version is recorded in their
catalog-info.yaml
Recording the Template Version in the Service
The scaffold writes the template version into the service's
catalog-info.yaml
as an annotation. This annotation is the basis for the drift detection and upgrade notification system.

# catalog-info.yaml  (generated by scaffold, checked into the service repo)
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: service-a
  annotations:
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
    scaffold.platform.io/template-name: genai-service
    scaffold.platform.io/template-version: "1.0.0"
    scaffold.platform.io/scaffolded-at: "2025-11-14"
spec:
  type: service
  owner: group:default/team-alpha
  lifecycle: production
Detecting Drift: Which Services Are Behind?

A nightly job queries the Backstage catalog for all components that carry the template annotation, compares their recorded version against the current template version, and produces a drift report used by the engineering scorecard and upgrade notification system.

# platform/template-governance/drift_detector.py
import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass
from packaging.version import Version

CATALOG_API = "https://backstage.internal/api/catalog"
CURRENT_TEMPLATE_VERSIONS = {
    "genai-service": "2.1.0",
    "genai-batch-job": "1.3.0",
}

@dataclass
class DriftReport:
    service: str
    owner: str
    template: str
    service_version: str
    current_version: str
    major_behind: int
    minor_behind: int
    action: str  # "required" | "recommended" | "none"

def fetch_all_services() -> list[dict]:
    resp = httpx.get(
        f"{CATALOG_API}/entities",
        params={
            "filter": "kind=Component,metadata.annotations.scaffold.platform.io/template-name",
            "fields": "metadata.name,metadata.annotations,spec.owner",
        },
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {BACKSTAGE_TOKEN}"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

def compute_drift(services: list[dict]) -> list[DriftReport]:
    reports = []
    for svc in services:
        annotations = svc["metadata"]["annotations"]
        template = annotations.get("scaffold.platform.io/template-name")
        svc_ver_str = annotations.get("scaffold.platform.io/template-version", "0.0.0")
        current_ver_str = CURRENT_TEMPLATE_VERSIONS.get(template)
        if not current_ver_str:
            continue

        svc_ver = Version(svc_ver_str)
        cur_ver = Version(current_ver_str)
        major_behind = cur_ver.major - svc_ver.major
        minor_behind = cur_ver.minor - svc_ver.minor if major_behind == 0 else 0

        if major_behind >= 2:
            action = "required"        # Scorecard penalty; 60-day deadline
        elif major_behind == 1:
            action = "recommended"     # Scorecard warning; 90-day window
        elif minor_behind >= 3:
            action = "recommended"     # 3+ minor versions behind
        else:
            action = "none"

        reports.append(DriftReport(
            service=svc["metadata"]["name"],
            owner=svc["spec"]["owner"],
            template=template,
            service_version=svc_ver_str,
            current_version=current_ver_str,
            major_behind=major_behind,
            minor_behind=minor_behind,
            action=action,
        ))
    return sorted(reports, key=lambda r: (r.major_behind, r.minor_behind), reverse=True)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    services = fetch_all_services()
    reports = compute_drift(services)
    required = [r for r in reports if r.action == "required"]
    recommended = [r for r in reports if r.action == "recommended"]
    print(f"Drift report: {len(required)} required, {len(recommended)} recommended upgrades")
    for r in reports:
        if r.action != "none":
            print(f"  {r.service} ({r.owner}): {r.service_version} → {r.current_version} [{r.action}]")
The Upgrade Notification Workflow

Platform teams should not wait for service owners to notice they are behind. The upgrade notification workflow opens GitHub PRs automatically for minor-version upgrades and sends Slack alerts for major-version drift.
Automated PR for Minor Upgrades

For minor version bumps, the platform team maintains a set of migration patches — small diffs that update the service's template annotation and add or modify the files introduced in the minor release. The notification job applies these patches and opens a PR in the service's repository.

# platform/template-governance/open_upgrade_pr.py
import httpx, base64, json
from pathlib import Path

def open_upgrade_pr(
    service_name: str,
    repo_slug: str,  # e.g. "myorg/service-a"
    from_version: str,
    to_version: str,
    patch_dir: Path,
) -> str:
    """Apply migration patch files and open a GitHub PR. Returns PR URL."""
    # Load patch files for this version transition
    patch_path = patch_dir / f"{from_version}-to-{to_version}"
    if not patch_path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"No patch for {from_version} → {to_version}")

    files_to_add = []
    for patch_file in patch_path.glob("**/*"):
        if patch_file.is_file():
            relative = patch_file.relative_to(patch_path)
            content = patch_file.read_bytes()
            files_to_add.append({
                "path": str(relative),
                "content": base64.b64encode(content).decode(),
                "encoding": "base64",
            })

    # Create branch and PR via GitHub API
    gh = httpx.Client(headers={
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
    }, base_url="https://api.github.com")

    # Get default branch SHA
    repo = gh.get(f"/repos/{repo_slug}").json()
    default_branch = repo["default_branch"]
    ref = gh.get(f"/repos/{repo_slug}/git/ref/heads/{default_branch}").json()
    base_sha = ref["object"]["sha"]

    # Create feature branch
    branch = f"platform/template-upgrade-{to_version}"
    gh.post(f"/repos/{repo_slug}/git/refs", json={
        "ref": f"refs/heads/{branch}",
        "sha": base_sha,
    })

    # Commit each patch file
    for f in files_to_add:
        gh.put(f"/repos/{repo_slug}/contents/{f['path']}", json={
            "message": f"chore(platform): template upgrade {from_version} → {to_version}",
            "content": f["content"],
            "branch": branch,
        })

    # Open PR
    pr = gh.post(f"/repos/{repo_slug}/pulls", json={
        "title": f"chore: upgrade scaffold template {from_version} → {to_version}",
        "head": branch,
        "base": default_branch,
        "body": (
            f"## Scaffold Template Upgrade\n\n"
            f"This PR upgrades your service from template **v{from_version}** to **v{to_version}**.\n\n"
            f"### What changed\n"
            f"See [template CHANGELOG](https://github.com/myorg/genai-template/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md"
            f"#v{to_version.replace('.', '')})\n\n"
            f"### Action required\n"
            f"- Review the diff\n"
            f"- Run the eval suite: `python -m src.evals.suite`\n"
            f"- Merge within 30 days to avoid scorecard penalty\n\n"
            f"_Opened by the platform template governance bot_"
        ),
    }).json()
    return pr["html_url"]
Slack Alert for Major Upgrades
Major version upgrades cannot be automated with a simple patch — they require human review. The notification job sends a Slack alert to the service owner's channel with a link to the migration guide.
Writing Migration Guides for Major Versions

Each major version of the template must ship with a migration guide. The guide is a Markdown file that lists every breaking change and provides the exact steps to apply each change to an existing service.

# Migration Guide: v1.x → v2.0.0

## Breaking changes

### 1. New eval harness structure

**Old:** No eval harness; teams wrote ad-hoc test scripts.

**New:** `src/evals/` directory with `suite.py`, `fixtures/`, and `judges/`.

**Steps:**
1. Create `src/evals/suite.py` — copy from the v2.0.0 skeleton:
   ```
   curl -sO https://raw.githubusercontent.com/myorg/genai-template/v2.0.0/skeleton/src/evals/suite.py
   mkdir -p src/evals/fixtures src/evals/judges
   ```
2. Add at least 3 fixtures to `src/evals/fixtures/smoke.jsonl`:
   ```jsonl
   {"id": "smoke-1", "input": {"query": "Hello"}, "expected_keywords": ["hello"], "must_not_contain": []}
   ```
3. Update `pyproject.toml` to add the `[eval]` extras group:
   ```toml
   [project.optional-dependencies]
   eval = ["pytest>=8.0", "httpx>=0.27"]
   ```
4. Add `eval_pass_threshold: 0.95` to `config/settings.yaml`.
5. Add `.github/workflows/eval.yml` — copy from v2.0.0 skeleton.

### 2. Catalog annotation required

**Old:** `catalog-info.yaml` had no template annotation.

**New:** Two annotations are required:
```yaml
annotations:
  scaffold.platform.io/template-name: genai-service
  scaffold.platform.io/template-version: "2.0.0"
```

Add these to your `catalog-info.yaml` metadata section.

### 3. Config schema change: `model` → `default_model`

**Old:** `config/settings.yaml` used key `model`.
**New:** Renamed to `default_model`.

```bash
sed -i 's/^model:/default_model:/' config/settings.yaml config/settings.*.yaml
```

Update any code that reads `settings.model` to `settings.default_model`.

## Verification checklist

- [ ] `python -m src.evals.suite --suite smoke` exits 0
- [ ] `mkdocs build --strict` exits 0
- [ ] `catalog-info.yaml` contains both template annotations
- [ ] `config/settings.yaml` uses `default_model` (not `model`)
Template Governance in the Engineering Scorecard

Template version currency is a scored dimension in the engineering scorecard. Teams that keep their template version current demonstrate platform alignment; those that fall significantly behind show that technical debt is accumulating.
Situation
Scorecard impact
Deadline
Up to date (0–1 minor behind)
No impact
N/A
2–3 minor versions behind
−5 points, Slack reminder weekly
30 days to merge opt-in PR
1 major version behind
−15 points, 'recommended' badge in catalog
90 days
2+ major versions behind
−30 points, 'action required' badge, engineering lead notified
60 days
Past deadline, no action
−50 points, escalated to VP Engineering
Immediate remediation required

The scorecard penalty creates a forcing function: teams cannot accumulate template debt indefinitely without it appearing in leadership reviews. Conversely, teams that consistently upgrade within the window gain a "Platform Champion" badge in Backstage, surfaced to leadership as a positive signal.
Pinning vs. Floating: When to Lock Template Versions
Services generally pin to the template version they were scaffolded from. Floating (always using the latest template) is not appropriate for service repositories because:
Major template changes can break builds if applied automatically without review
Minor template changes may introduce new required config that must be supplied before the service starts
Audit trails require knowing exactly which template version was used when a service was scaffolded
The recommended model is
pin on scaffold, upgrade via PR

: the service records the scaffold version at creation time, and platform automation opens upgrade PRs on a defined cadence. Teams review and merge — they are never surprised by automatic template changes in their repository.
Tooling: Template Version CLI
Platform teams can provide a CLI tool that service owners run locally to check their upgrade status and preview the changes an upgrade would apply:

# Check current drift status
$ platform-tools template-drift --service service-a
Service: service-a
Template: genai-service
Your version: 1.0.0
Current version: 2.1.0
Status: ACTION REQUIRED (2 major versions behind)
Migration guide: https://github.com/myorg/genai-template/blob/main/docs/migration/v1-to-v2.md

# Preview changes for a specific upgrade
$ platform-tools template-upgrade --service service-a --to 2.1.0 --dry-run
Would add:
  src/evals/suite.py
  src/evals/fixtures/smoke.jsonl
  .github/workflows/eval.yml
  config/eval-config/thresholds.yaml

Would modify:
  catalog-info.yaml  (+2 annotations)
  config/settings.yaml  (model → default_model)
  pyproject.toml  (+eval extras group)

No files would be deleted.
Run without --dry-run to apply changes.

# Apply the upgrade
$ platform-tools template-upgrade --service service-a --to 2.1.0
Applied 3 additions, 3 modifications.
Run your eval suite to verify: python -m src.evals.suite --suite smoke
Summary
Template versioning closes the loop between a living platform standard and the services it governs. The key decisions are:
Semantic versioning for templates: patch = fix, minor = additive, major = breaking — applied to scaffold conventions, not code
Record the template version in catalog-info.yaml at scaffold time — this is the source of truth for drift detection
Automated drift detection: nightly job compares catalog annotations against current template version; produces scored drift report
Automated minor-version upgrade PRs: platform tooling applies migration patches and opens PRs so teams can review and merge
Migration guides for major versions: exact steps, verification checklist, and deadline — shipped alongside the major release
Engineering scorecard integration: template currency is a scored dimension with escalating penalties for deferred upgrades

When these mechanisms are in place, platform improvements propagate to all services within a bounded time window rather than only benefiting newly scaffolded teams — compounding the platform's investment in every template update.
